quality_note: All records use one candidate quality standard; no difficulty labels are assigned.

records.title: The Sidewalk's Smallest Rebellion
records.observable_target: The 500-square-inch triangular mosaic embedded in the public sidewalk, asserting that the Hess estate never dedicated it for public use.
records.quoted_evidence: “The Hess triangle is a triangular, 500-square-inch ... plot of private land in the middle of a public sidewalk at the corner of Seventh Avenue and Christopher Street”; it is “covered by a mosaic plaque” reading “PROPERTY OF THE HESS ESTATE WHICH HAS NEVER BEEN DEDICATED FOR PUBLIC PURPOSES.”
records.riddle: The city took a whole block, but one tiny corner refused to surrender. Find the rebellion you could cover with a shopping bag.
hints: Look down where Christopher Street meets Seventh Avenue.
Find the tiny triangular mosaic declaring itself private property.
records.reveal_concept: A property dispute resolves into an almost comically small piece of land that is still visibly claimed underfoot.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: View from the public sidewalk without blocking the corner or adjacent doorway. Do not require touching or standing on the mosaic; use signalized crossings. Sidewalk grade and crowd clearance need field audit.
records.durability_risk: Low-medium: the mosaic has survived since 1922, but sidewalk work, coverings, queues, or adjacent-business operations can obstruct it.

records.title: Four Figures, Two Quiet Conversations
records.canonical_place: Gay Liberation by George Segal, Christopher Park
records.observable_target: George Segal's four white figures: two standing men and two seated women in relaxed poses.
records.quoted_evidence: “The statue consists of four white figures (two standing men and two seated women) positioned in ‘natural, easy’ poses.”
records.riddle: Two stand, two sit, and all four turn an ordinary pause into a public declaration.
hints: Search the fenced green opposite the Stonewall buildings.
Find George Segal's four white figures: two men standing and two women seated.
records.reveal_concept: The player recognizes liberation not as a heroic solo figure but as two calm, everyday same-sex pairs.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: Observe from Christopher Park paths during posted hours; do not climb, touch, or stage intrusive photographs with visitors. Confirm a step-free sightline and whether fencing or foliage limits the view.
records.durability_risk: Low-medium: permanent public art, with possible conservation barriers, park closures, snow, or foliage obstruction.

records.title: A Sky Made of Triangles
records.observable_target: The 18-foot steel canopy assembled from large triangles filled with smaller triangular patterns, above a granite panel engraved with lines from Walt Whitman's Song of Myself.
records.quoted_evidence: The memorial “consists of an 18-foot ... high steel canopy”; “Each of the triangles is filled with 16 smaller triangles”; the built memorial “features the work of visual artist Jenny Holzer” engraving granite with lines from Walt Whitman's “Song of Myself.”
records.riddle: Triangles multiply overhead while a poet speaks from stone underfoot. Find the open shelter built for remembrance.
hints: Look on the triangular island where Greenwich Avenue meets Seventh Avenue and 12th Street.
Find the 18-foot steel canopy and Whitman's words in granite.
records.reveal_concept: Geometry and poetry share one memorial: a lattice overhead and a text-based artwork below.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: Use the memorial's public paved approach; do not require reading the full floor text or distinguishing color. Avoid blocking circulation and treat mourners respectfully. Confirm step-free access, glare, and wet-surface conditions.
records.durability_risk: Low: permanent memorial; medium playability risk from events, maintenance, snow, glare, or temporary barriers.

records.title: The Island Growing from 132 Tulips
records.observable_target: The elevated public park supported over the Hudson by 132 pot-shaped concrete structures called tulips.
records.quoted_evidence: “Little Island covers 2.4 acres ... and is supported by 132 pot-shaped structures (called ‘tulips’) suspended above the water”; the park is connected by footbridges at 13th and 14th Streets.
records.riddle: A garden floats above the Hudson, but its 132 tulips are concrete and bloom beneath the soil.
hints: Search offshore between the 13th- and 14th-Street footbridges.
Look beneath the park for 132 pot-shaped supports called tulips.
records.reveal_concept: The apparent island is read from below as an engineered bouquet holding up a real landscape.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: Use Hudson River Park paths or Little Island's public footbridges during posted hours. Never lean over barriers or enter shoreline/service areas. Confirm the best step-free exterior viewpoint that reveals multiple supports without requiring park entry.
records.durability_risk: Low for structure; medium for hours, event closures, crowding, river fog, and seasonal vegetation.

records.title: A Manhattan Beach Where Swimming Stops
records.observable_target: The Hudson-edge public beachfront made with 1,200 short tons of sand, paired with a sunning lawn and kayak landing, where swimming and wading are prohibited.
records.quoted_evidence: The park “includes a beach with 1,200 short tons ... of sand”; amenities include “a sports field, sunning lawn, promenades, two dog runs, a picnic area, fitness equipment, and a beachfront kayak launch,” although “swimming and wading are not permitted.”
records.riddle: Twelve hundred tons of sand made Manhattan a beach, yet your feet must stop where the river begins.
hints: Head to the Hudson at the foot of Gansevoort Street.
Find the sandy public beachfront where swimming and wading are not allowed.
records.reveal_concept: A genuine beach texture appears in Manhattan, but the satisfying twist is that it is for shore life and launching—not swimming.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: Stay on public park surfaces and obey all no-swimming/no-wading signs. Do not require stepping onto loose sand; provide a paved viewing alternative. Audit seasonal hours, accessible routes, and shoreline barriers.
records.durability_risk: Medium: permanent park identity, but sand level, maintenance, storms, closures, and operating rules can change.

records.title: The One-Block Echo of a Ferry
records.observable_target: The unusually short lane, one block from and parallel to the Hudson, running only between Christopher and West 10th Streets.
records.quoted_evidence: “Weehawken Street is a short street ... one block from and parallel to West and Washington Streets, running between Christopher Street and West 10th Street.”
records.riddle: I borrow New Jersey's name, shadow the Hudson for one short block, then vanish before Manhattan can make me an avenue.
hints: Search near the waterfront between Christopher and West 10th Streets.
Find the short lane named for the old connection to Weehawken.
records.reveal_concept: The clue resolves into a tiny surviving street whose name still points across the river.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: Complete from public sidewalks; do not enter buildings, alleys, or loading zones. The lane is narrow, so field-check curb cuts, truck activity, pavement, and a safe endpoint view.
records.durability_risk: Low for street plan and name; medium for construction, parked vehicles, and loading activity obscuring the one-block perspective.

records.title: The Park Cut by Old Footpaths
records.observable_target: The tiny triangular park formed where Greenwich Avenue, Horatio Street, and Eighth Avenue meet.
records.quoted_evidence: The 0.227-acre park is bordered by Eighth Avenue, Horatio Street, and Greenwich Avenue; “Its triangular shape arose from the intersection of two Native American footpaths that grew into Greenwich Village streets.”
records.riddle: Two old paths crossed, the grid arrived late, and a tiny green triangle was left holding the argument.
hints: Look where Greenwich Avenue and Eighth Avenue nearly pinch together.
Find the triangular park bordered also by Horatio Street.
records.reveal_concept: The park's shape makes layers of movement legible without requiring a plaque-reading task.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: Observe from within the public park or surrounding sidewalks using marked crossings. Do not require standing in a traffic island or roadway. Confirm step-free gates, hours, and the clearest triangle view.
records.durability_risk: Low for the street geometry; medium for foliage, renovation, temporary fencing, or events.

records.title: A Quarter-Acre with Four Street Edges
records.observable_target: The exceptionally small 0.25-acre park enclosed among Eighth Avenue, Bank Street, Hudson Street, and West 12th Street.
records.quoted_evidence: The park is bordered by “Eighth Avenue, Bank Street, Hudson Street and West 12th Street” and, “at 0.25 acres,” is “one of [New York City's] smallest.”
records.riddle: Four streets frame me, but a single city block could swallow me four times over. Find the pocket of green they could not erase.
hints: Search near the meeting of Hudson Street and Eighth Avenue.
Find the quarter-acre park also edged by Bank and West 12th Streets.
records.reveal_concept: A size riddle becomes a compact neighborhood garden defined by four surrounding streets.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: Use park gates during posted hours or view from a public sidewalk. Cross only at marked points; confirm step-free entry, gate widths, seating circulation, and whether plantings obscure the small-scale read.
records.durability_risk: Low for park identity and footprint; medium for seasonal planting, locked gates, renovation, and events.

records.title: Cupcakes at the First Corner
records.canonical_place: Magnolia Bakery, original Bleecker Street location
records.observable_target: The original Magnolia Bakery corner location at 401 Bleecker Street, identifiable as the bakery known for cupcakes and banana pudding.
records.quoted_evidence: “The first location opened in 1996 at 401 Bleecker Street, on the corner of West 11th Street”; the bakery is known “especially [for] its cupcakes and banana pudding.”
records.riddle: Before this bakery multiplied around the world, cupcakes and pudding began their craze at one Bleecker Street corner.
hints: Go to the corner of Bleecker and West 11th Streets.
Find the original 1996 Magnolia Bakery location.
records.reveal_concept: The global brand resolves back to a specific neighborhood corner and two signature sweets.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: Exterior completion only; no purchase or queueing required. Do not block the entrance or line. Field-check current branding visibility, curb ramps, sidewalk congestion, and an accessible completion radius.
records.durability_risk: Medium: longstanding original location, but business continuity, branding, awnings, and queues can change.

records.title: West Meets Bethune
records.observable_target: The full-block artists' complex whose portmanteau name joins its West Street and Bethune Street edges.
records.quoted_evidence: The complex “comprises the full city block bounded by West, Bethune, Washington and Bank Streets”; “the complex is named for the streets West and Bethune.”
records.riddle: My name is a street-corner handshake: the first half faces the river, the second honors Bethune, and artists fill the block between.
hints: Look for an artists' community occupying a full block near the Hudson.
Join the names West and Bethune to make the answer.
records.reveal_concept: Players solve a place-name construction and then confirm its physical full-block footprint.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: Use only public perimeter sidewalks; do not enter residential courtyards or photograph residents. Confirm which public-facing name marker and corner view make the wordplay independently observable and step-free.
records.durability_risk: Low for institution, name, and block footprint; medium for signage visibility, facade work, and residential privacy constraints.

records.title: The Visitor Center Beside the Bar
records.observable_target: The official national-monument visitor center at 51 Christopher Street immediately beside the present Stonewall Inn at number 53.
records.quoted_evidence: The visitor center was built “at 51 Christopher Street” and “opened on June 28, 2024, as the first official national visitors center dedicated to the LGBTQ+ experience”; the Stonewall Inn source identifies the bar at 53 Christopher Street.
records.riddle: One address keeps pouring; the next now welcomes the nation. Find 51 and 53 sharing the same story.
hints: Look on Christopher Street opposite the small national-monument park.
Find the visitor center at 51 beside the Stonewall Inn at 53.
records.reveal_concept: The historic site is read as a contemporary pair: active bar and public interpretation center side by side.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: Exterior-only completion from the public sidewalk; visitor-center entry must be optional and hours-independent. Do not block either entrance or photograph patrons without consent. Audit crowding and curb access.
records.durability_risk: Low-medium: federal monument and newly opened center are stable, but exterior branding, security measures, and hours may change.

records.title: A Stone Fountain in a Traffic-Made Triangle
records.observable_target: The current stone fountain inside the quarter-acre triangular piazza bounded by Sixth Avenue, Bleecker Street, and Carmine Street.
records.quoted_evidence: Father Demo Square is a “0.25-acre ... triangular park and piazza bounded by Sixth Avenue, Bleecker Street, and Carmine Street”; a 2007 renovation installed “the current stone fountain.”
records.riddle: Three streets cut the piazza; one stone fountain gives its center back.
hints: Search the South Village where Bleecker and Carmine meet Sixth Avenue.
Find the triangular Father Demo Square and its stone fountain.
records.reveal_concept: Busy street geometry resolves into a small civic room organized around one durable object.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: Enter by public paved paths or observe from the perimeter using marked crossings. Do not step into traffic for a symmetrical view. Confirm fountain operation is not necessary, plus curb ramps and seating clearance.
records.durability_risk: Low for fountain and footprint; medium for water operation, winterization, events, and maintenance barriers.

records.title: The Fountain Where the City Gathers
records.observable_target: The park's central Tisch Fountain and circular gathering area, one of its two prominent features and a longstanding popular spot.
records.quoted_evidence: “The park's fountain area has long been one of the city's popular spots”; “The two prominent features are the Washington Square Arch and the Tisch Fountain.”
records.riddle: I am not the marble gateway, yet crowds orbit me at the Village's center. Find the park's other famous circle.
hints: Go toward the middle of Washington Square Park, not its north entrance.
Find the Tisch Fountain and its circular gathering area.
records.reveal_concept: The clue redirects attention from the famous arch to the social center that people gather around.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: Use park paths during posted hours. Completion must not require entering the basin, touching water, or fountain operation. Confirm a step-free approach and an alternate completion radius during performances or crowding.
records.durability_risk: Low for fountain structure; medium for seasonal shutdown, maintenance, events, and dense crowds.

records.title: Queens and Rooks in the Open Air
records.canonical_place: Chess and Scrabble playing area, Washington Square Park
records.observable_target: The park's dedicated outdoor chess and Scrabble playing area.
records.quoted_evidence: Washington Square Park “includes ... a chess and scrabble playing area,” along with benches and picnic tables.
records.riddle: No roof, no hushed library: here queens fall, words cross, and strangers meet across stone tables.
hints: Search the southwest side of Washington Square Park.
Find the dedicated chess and Scrabble playing area.
records.reveal_concept: A public-game landscape turns ordinary tables into one of the park's strongest neighborhood-life signatures.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: Observe without interrupting games, occupying a needed seat, or photographing identifiable players without consent. Provide a perimeter completion option; field-check table height, path access, and crowding.
records.durability_risk: Low for dedicated area; medium for renovation, tournaments, weather, or all tables being occupied.

records.title: The Italian Soldier Among the Buskers
records.canonical_place: Giuseppe Garibaldi statue, Washington Square Park
records.observable_target: The commemorative statue of Italian patriot and soldier Giuseppe Garibaldi within Washington Square Park.
records.quoted_evidence: Park statuary commemorates “Italian patriot and soldier Giuseppe Garibaldi, commander of the insurrectionist forces in Italy's struggle for unification.”
records.riddle: An Italian commander crossed an ocean only in bronze; now he keeps watch where musicians gather.
hints: Search among the commemorative figures inside Washington Square Park.
Find the statue of Italian patriot Giuseppe Garibaldi.
records.reveal_concept: A named international figure emerges from a park better known for its arch, fountain, and performers.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: View from park paths; no climbing or touching. The clue cannot depend on reading small inscriptions. Field-check foliage, sightline, step-free route, and differentiation from other statues.
records.durability_risk: Low: permanent monument; medium playability risk from foliage, gatherings, conservation work, or snow.

records.title: Colored Glass on the South Side of the Square
records.observable_target: The church complex opposite Washington Square, especially the sanctuary's stained-glass windows by John La Farge and its separate campanile tower.
records.quoted_evidence: The sanctuary, campanile tower, and Judson Hall are designated landmarks; the church “was designed by architect Stanford White, with stained glass windows by John La Farge.”
records.riddle: A White building holds La Farge color, while its bell tower stands watch across from the square.
hints: Look along Washington Square South for a separate campanile.
Find Judson Memorial Church and the stained glass credited to John La Farge.
records.reveal_concept: Two artist surnames become fair clue mechanics for a church where architecture and visual art meet.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: Exterior completion only unless public visibility of the glass is inadequate; do not require worship-space entry or disrupt services. Field-check whether the windows are identifiable from a legal step-free public viewpoint and in varied light.
records.durability_risk: Low for tower and windows; medium for scaffolding, foliage, lighting, events, and uncertain exterior visibility of the glass.

records.title: Trace the Buried Water
records.observable_target: The bent alignment of Minetta Lane and the one-block Minetta Street, which follow part of the former Minetta Creek's course before curving toward Sixth Avenue.
records.quoted_evidence: Minetta Creek “flowed southward across modern-day Washington Square Park” and “crossed southwest along the present route of Minetta Lane”; it then “roughly followed the current path of Minetta Street, a one-block road ... before curving southwest to meet Sixth Avenue.”
records.riddle: The water disappeared underground, but two little streets still bend as if the current were choosing their route.
hints: Begin southwest of Washington Square and look for the repeated name Minetta.
Follow Minetta Lane into one-block Minetta Street as it curves toward Sixth Avenue.
records.reveal_concept: A vanished creek becomes visible through surviving street geometry rather than a plaque or historical date.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: Stay on public sidewalks and use marked crossings; no basement, sewer, or private-property access. Field-check pavement, curb ramps, traffic, and whether the curve is legible without standing in the roadway.
records.durability_risk: Low for street names and alignment; medium for construction, parked vehicles, and weak visual legibility at ground level.

records.title: The Jazz Note That Became a World Map
records.canonical_place: Blue Note Jazz Club, New York flagship
records.observable_target: The publicly identifiable flagship jazz-club frontage at 131 West 3rd Street, the original location of a now-international venue network.
records.quoted_evidence: Blue Note was “originally established in Greenwich Village”; “the flagship location remains at 131 West 3rd Street,” while the brand operates in cities across North America, South America, Europe, and Asia.
records.riddle: Tokyo, Rio, Milan, and Beijing echo me, but the first note still sounds on West 3rd Street.
hints: Look for a jazz venue just south of Washington Square.
Find the Blue Note flagship at 131 West 3rd Street.
records.reveal_concept: An international chain is solved through its still-operating Greenwich Village flagship.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: Exterior completion only; no ticket, entry, or purchase required. Do not block the box office, entrance, or performers. Field-check current exterior identification, nighttime lighting dependence, sidewalk clearance, and curb access.
records.durability_risk: Medium: flagship identity is longstanding, but branding, operations, show queues, and exterior visibility can change.

records.title: A Museum Disguised as Three Houses
records.matched_catalog_name: Whitney Museum of American Art (original building)
records.canonical_place: Original Whitney Museum building / New York Studio School
records.observable_target: The joined collection of three 1838 rowhouses at 8–12 West 8th Street that formed the Whitney's original building.
records.quoted_evidence: “The Whitney Museum of American Art's original building is a collection of three 1838 rowhouses at 8–12 West 8th Street.”
records.riddle: Before the museum learned to look like a museum, it hid in three neighbors born in the same year.
hints: Search West 8th Street between Fifth Avenue and MacDougal Street.
Find the three 1838 rowhouses that became the original Whitney Museum.
records.reveal_concept: The player has to read one cultural institution as three domestic facades joined into a whole.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: Exterior sidewalk observation only; do not require school entry. Avoid blocking doors or photographing interiors. Field-check whether all three rowhouses can be distinguished from a step-free legal viewpoint despite trees and scaffolding.
records.durability_risk: Low for landmarked building group; medium for foliage, parked vehicles, facade work, and visual ambiguity between the three houses.

records.title: The One-Block Street That Refuses the Grid
records.observable_target: The street's short, angled, one-block course through the Village grid.
records.quoted_evidence: “Gay Street is a short, angled street that marks off one block of Greenwich Village.”
records.riddle: I last only one block and still refuse to walk straight with Manhattan's grid.
hints: Look west of Seventh Avenue South near Christopher Street.
Find the short, angled, one-block Gay Street.
records.reveal_concept: The answer is pure urban shape: a street too short and crooked to behave like the surrounding grid.
records.public_approach_safety_mobility: Remain on public sidewalks; do not stage photos in the roadway or linger at private stoops. Field-check curb ramps, grade, vehicle visibility, and a safe viewpoint where the angle reads clearly.
records.durability_risk: Low for street name and geometry; medium for parked vehicles, construction, and the difficulty of perceiving the angle from one endpoint.

rejected_notes.reason: The source supports the isosceles-triangle basement, but verification would likely require private interior entry and a paid performance; no reliable exterior equivalent was sourced.

rejected_notes.candidate: Electric Lady Studios round windows and colored ambient lighting
rejected_notes.reason: The sourced details describe the private studio interior; current legal public visibility was not established.

rejected_notes.candidate: Julius' Jacob Ruppert barrel tables and wall photographs
rejected_notes.reason: Distinctive and sourced, but depends on entry to a bar, changing interior placement, age restrictions, and respectful patron behavior.
The display is seasonal and event timing is volatile; the accepted concept uses the durable park footprint instead.
Swimming and wading are explicitly prohibited; the accepted concept treats the prohibition as context and keeps players on legal park surfaces.

rejected_notes.candidate: Minetta Creek water sounds or visible basement flow
rejected_notes.reason: No reliable public sensory manifestation was established; the accepted concept uses only sourced street alignment.
The location and food identity are sourced, but current colors and lettering were not; the accepted concept avoids inventing them.

rejected_notes.candidate: White Horse Tavern neon horse sign
rejected_notes.reason: The tavern identity is stable, but an exact authoritative text source for the current exterior sign configuration was not established in this research pass.
